title: REGIONAL STANDARD FOR YACON Latin America and the Caribbean

sections.section: REGIONAL STANDARD FOR YACON Latin America and the Caribbean
sections.summary: Heading only: Regional Standard for Yacon for Latin America and the Caribbean.
Document identifier: CXS 324R-2017.
Adoption note indicating the standard was adopted in 2017 and amended in 2023.
Amendment notice stating that following the 2023 Codex Commission decisions, Section 7.2 on labelling of non‑retail containers was amended.

Defines the scope: sets quality requirements for yacon at the export‑control stage after preparation and packaging, notes that if the standard is applied after packaging product may show slight lack of freshness/turgidity or slight deterioration for lower classes, and places responsibility on the holder/seller to ensure conformity and not market non‑conforming produce.
Defines the produce covered: tuberous roots of Smallanthus sonchifolius (yacon) intended to be supplied fresh after preparation and packaging, excluding yacon for industrial processing.
Introduces the quality provisions for yacon, including minimum requirements, maturity, and classification into defined classes with associated tolerances.

Lists minimum quality requirements (intact, sound, clean, practically free from pests and mechanical damage, fresh appearance, firm, no abnormal moisture, no foreign smell/taste) and specifies dimensional/finish limits (distal cut ≤2 cm diameter; stalk end cut 1–2.5 cm where present) and that produce must withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.
Requires yacon to have reached an appropriate degree of maturity for trade and consumption consistent with the variety/commercial type and growing area.
States that yacon is classified into three classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) as defined in subsequent provisions.

Defines Extra Class: yacon of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type, free from defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I yacon must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type, with only slight defects allowed that do not affect general appearance, quality, keeping quality or package presentation—permitted defects: slight shape defects, scarring or healed damage up to 10% of surface, and scraped areas up to 10% of surface—and defects must not affect the pulp.

Class II covers yacon that fail to qualify for higher classes but meet minimum requirements, allowing defects (shape defects, scarring/healed damage up to 20% of surface, scraped areas up to 20% of surface) provided essential quality, keeping quality and presentation remain and the flesh is free of major quality defects.

Yacon may be sized by maximum equatorial diameter and by weight and packages must be labelled with an optional size code table (code 1: >70 mm and >300 g; code 2: >50–70 mm and >120–300 g; code 3: 30–50 mm and ≤120 g), and all sizes may have fusiform, oval/ovate, obovate or irregular forms.

At all marketing stages tolerances by lot are allowed: Extra class permits 5% (number or weight) not meeting the class but meeting Class I; Class I permits 10% not meeting the class but meeting Class II; Class II permits 10% neither meeting the class nor the minimum (excluding produce rotten or otherwise unfit); for sized lots a 10% size tolerance (number or weight) applies.
At all marketing stages, tolerances in respect of quality and size shall be allowed in each lot for produce not satisfying the requirements of the class indicated.
Extra class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of yacon in the lot may fail to satisfy Extra class requirements provided they meet Class I.
Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of yacon in the lot may fail to satisfy Class I requirements provided they meet Class II.

Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of yacon in the lot may neither satisfy Class II nor the minimum requirements, excluding produce affected by rotting or other deterioration rendering them unfit for consumption.
Size tolerances: for all classes when sized, up to 10% by number or weight of yacon in the lot may not satisfy the sizing requirements.
Heading only — general provisions concerning presentation (no substantive requirements in this section text).

Packages or lots must be uniform in form and contain only yacon of the same origin, variety/commercial type, colour, quality and size, with the visible part representative of the whole; mixtures of different varieties/commercial types are allowed only if uniform in quality and of the same origin.

Yacon must be packed to protect the produce using food-grade, clean materials that do not cause external or internal damage, with printed labels/stamps using non-toxic ink/glue, stickers that leave no visible glue or skin defects, and packaging in compliance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruit and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).
Containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of yacon and must be free of foreign matter and odour.

Labelling requirements: consumer packages must meet CXS 119-1985 plus specific provisions on nature (name and optional variety) and origin (country and optionally district/place, with each country shown for mixtures of varieties of different origins); non-retail containers should follow CXS 346-2021 and must indicate produce name/variety and, for mixtures not visible from outside, list varieties/types/colours, with traceability in line with CXG 60-2006.
Introductory heading only — states that specific consumer-package labelling provisions follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 119-1985).
Each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may be labelled with the variety and/or commercial type.

Origin labelling: country of origin must be indicated and may include district/place name, and for mixtures of distinctly different varieties from different origins each country of origin must be shown next to the variety/commercial type name.
Introductory heading only — non-retail container labelling should follow CXS 346-2021, with additional specific requirements provided in the subsequent subsection.

Non-retail labelling: name of the produce and optional variety/commercial type must appear, distinctly different varieties/commercial types in a mixture must be named, and mixtures of varieties/types/colours not visible from outside must indicate those varieties/types/colours; the full or commonly used name should be used and the product should allow traceability per CXG 60-2006.

Food additives: because yacon is classified in food category 04.2.1.1 'untreated fresh vegetables…roots and tubers', no food additives are allowed in accordance with the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995).

Requires the product covered by the standard to comply with Codex pesticide maximum residue limits and the maximum levels for contaminants and toxins set out in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995).

Recommends that produce be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53‑2003) and other relevant Codex hygienic texts.

Bibliographic notes listing referenced FAO/WHO/Codex documents (packaging, labelling, additives, contaminants, hygiene, microbiological criteria, etc.) and a note that Codex members in the Latin America and Caribbean region are indicated on the Codex website; non‑substantive reference material.
